titleStmt.title: Bipolaronic superconductivity out of a Coulomb gas

analytic.title: Bipolaronic superconductivity out of a Coulomb gas

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Employing unbiased sign-problem-free quantum Monte Carlo, we investigate the effects of longrange Coulomb forces on BEC of bipolarons using a model of bond phonon-modulated electron hopping. In absence of long-range repulsion, this model was recently shown to give rise to small-size, light-mass bipolarons that undergo a superfluid transition at high values of the critical transition temperature Tc. We find that Tc in our model even with the long-range Coulomb repulsion remains much larger than that of Holstein bipolarons, and can be on the order of or greater than the typical upper bounds on phonon-mediated Tc based on the Migdal-Eliashberg and McMillan approximations. Our work points to a physically simple mechanism for superconductivity in the low-density regime that may be relevant to current experiments on dilute superconductors.

Introduction. Understanding the mechanisms of superconductivity in the dilute density regime is an active theme of research, relevant to polar materials [1,2], doped topological insulators [3,4], transition metal dichalcogenides [5], moiré materials [6][7][8][9][10][11] and other materials [12]. This large and growing list of ultra-low carrier density superconductors motivates theoretical examination of superconductivity in electron-phonon coupled systems at very small densities where, as a matter of principle, the Fermi liquid/Migdal-Eliashberg paradigm must fail. Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) of preformed pairs ("bipolarons") in principle offers a robust route to superconductivity at low densities. But, in the low-density regime, the Coulomb repulsion is weakly screened and thus the pairing "glue" required to bind electron pairs into bound states must be strong enough to overcome the Coulomb repulsion. A strong pairing interaction is usually believed to result in heavy bound states, implying low values of the critical transition temperature T c . These considerations [13,14] are widely believed to severely limit the maximum T c obtained from phonon-mediated binding of electrons into bipolarons.

We have recently shown [15] that even in the presence of a short-ranged interaction parameterized by a large onsite Hubbard repulsion U , electrons coupled to phonons via bond phonon-modulated electron hopping form small-size, light-mass bipolarons [16,17] that undergo a superfluid ("BEC") transition at values of T c that are much larger than those obtained in (Holstein) models in which the electron density is coupled to phonons or from Migdal-Eliashberg theory of superconductivity out of a Fermi liquid. This work did not include the longrange part of the Coulomb interaction, so is relevant to two-dimensional (2D) materials in which the Coulomb repulsion is completely screened by gating or proximity to a substrate [15]. However, in ungated 2D materials and in three-dimensional (3D) materials in which the Coulomb repulsion cannot be screened by an external gate, the question of the effects of long-range Coulomb repulsion on bipolaronic superconductivity (and other nonphononic BEC mechanisms [18][19][20][21][22]) remains open.

In this letter, we study BEC of bipolarons occuring in a dilute, 3D Coulomb gas, showing that T c of bondcoupled bipolarons is still higher than that of densitycoupled (Holstein) bipolarons, and in line with the value of T c typically found in experiments on 3D materials believed to be close to or in the low-density regime. In a Coulomb system, the two-electron bound state retains a finite size even at the critical interaction strength associated with unbinding [23], and thus the maximum T c is determined by a combination of binding strength, mass and size with the constraint that the size cannot be infinite. To the best of our knowledge, despite decades of debate [13,24,25], our theory is the first quantitative effort that takes the presence of long-range Coulomb interaction into account and (i) demonstrates, using an unbiased approach, a realistic mechanism for BEC formation at relatively high values of T c and (ii) unveils the properties of bipolarons, e.g. their mass and size, in 3D.
Model. We consider the bond-Peierls [26] (also known as bond-Su-Schrieffer-Heeger [27]) electron-phonon coupling on a 3D cubic lattice. In this model the electronic
div.formula: T c /Ω U/t = 8 V = U/10 t/Ω = 1 t/Ω = 2 t/Ω = 2 ≈ bP H FIG. 1.
div.p: Estimates of Tc of the bond-Peierls (bP) bipolaronic superconductor (filled squares, blue lines) for different t/Ω at U = 8t, V(r > 0) = V /r with V = U/10 as a function of λ computed according to Eq. ( 3) from QMC simulations of the bipolaron effective mass m BP and its mean squared-radius R 2 BP . We contrast this to superconductivity of Holstein (H) bipolarons (open circles, orange line) at t/Ω = 2 for the same values of U/t and V . Here we use λ = α 2 /3Ωt for bond-Peierls bipolarons, λ = 0.85α 2 /6Ωt for Holstein bipolarons (we use a factor of 0.85 so that the two sets of data can be presented on the same scale of λ). The doubly wavy symbol indicates absence of bipolarons in the Holstein model for λ 0.91 where a crossover from BEC to BCS may occur.
hopping between two sites is modulated by a single oscillator centered on the bond connecting the two sites [15]. The Hamiltonian is

Here, the lattice Coulomb model for electrons with spin σ ∈ {↑, ↓} is Ĥe = -t i,j ,σ ĉ † i,σ ĉj,σ + h.c. + U i ni,↑ ni,↓ +(1/2) i =j V i,j ni nj with nearest-neighbor (NN) hopping t, onsite Hubbard repulsion U , NN repulsion V and longer-range repulsion V ij = V a |ri-rj | , where ni = ni,↑ + ni,↓ with ni,σ = ĉ † i,σ ĉi,σ at site r i , and a is the lattice constant (NN distance). The notation i, j refers to NN sites. Writing the Coulomb repulsion as e 2 / r (where the dielectric constant encodes short-ranged polarization effects arising from degrees of freedom not explicitly included in the model) we estimate the onsite U = e 2 / a orb with a orb the unit cell orbital size, the NN V = e 2 / a ≈ U a orb /a, and further-neighbor (distance r > a) V r>a = V a/r. We henceforth set the lattice constant a = 1. We model distortions of the bonds connecting sites i and j as Einstein oscillators with Hamiltonian Ĥph = i,j
div.formula: 1 2 K X2 i,j + P 2 i,j /2M = Ω i,j
div.p: b † i,j bi,j with oscillator frequency ( = 1) Ω = K/M . The interaction between electrons and phonons takes the form
div.formula: Ve-ph = α √ 2M Ω i,j ,σ ĉ † i,σ ĉj,σ + h.c. Xi,j(2)
div.p: describing the modulation of electron hopping by an oscillator Xi,j := bond connecting sites i and j with coupling coefficient α √ 2M Ω. We set M = 1. The relevant parameters are a dimensionless coupling constant λ = ((α √ 2Ω) 2 /K)/6t = α 2 /(3Ωt), the ratio of the typical polaronic energy scale to the free electron energy scale, and an adiabaticity parameter t/Ω. It is important to note that a typical physical origin for this phonon-modulated hopping is from interference of different hopping pathways [15]. This implies that the magnitude of the coupling term (Eq. ( 2)) is independent of the bare hopping, which means that the model remains valid in the strong-coupling regime even when the electronic hopping changes sign. This is different from other models of phonon-modulated hopping [16,[28][29][30][31][32] in which an equation of the general form of Eq. (2) applies only in the small displacement regime where the net change in hopping amplitude is small relative to the bare hopping [33].

Method. Using a numerically exact sign-problem-free quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) approach based on a pathintegral formulation of the electronic sector combined with either a real-space diagrammatic or a Fock-space path-integral representation of the phononic sector [17] we study singlet bipolaron formation in the two-electron sector of the model. We simulate the model on a 3D cubic lattice with linear size L = 140 sites and open boundary conditions. This system size is large enough to access the thermodynamic limit and eliminate boundary effects. We expect that our results are qualitatively similar for other models in which the hopping is modulated by phonons.

BEC of bipolarons. In 3D, a dilute system of electrons, at strong enough electron-phonon coupling, is unstable to the formation of bipolarons and thus forms a gas of interacting bosons. Competing instabilities such as phase separation [34] or Wigner crystallization [35] are very unlikely in three dimensions in a Coulomb system unless the density is extremely low. Thus, based on recent results on BEC out of a Coulomb Bose plasma [36], bipolarons will undergo condensation into a BEC and superfluidity at a T ≤ T c with T c ≈ 3.2ρ

is the bipolaron effective mass, with E BP (K) the bipolaron dispersion and K the bipolaron momentum. This estimate remains reliable in a broad density range up to the density at which bipolarons overlap. The largest T c from this mechanism is thus expected to arise around the density ρ BP = min{(1/( 4 3 πR 3 BP ), 1/( 4 3 π)} at which the inter-particle separation becomes on the order of the bipolaron radial size R BP , which, after lattice regularization, must be at least unity. This leads to an estimate of T c of the bipolaron superconductor at the overlap density that depends only on the bipolaron properties given by
div.formula: T c ≈ 1.2 m BP R 2 BP if R 2 BP ≥ 1 1.2 m BP otherwise,(3)
div.p: where R 2 BP := Ψ BP | R2 |Ψ BP is the bipolaron mean squared-radius, with Ψ BP the bipolaron ground state wavefunction.

Bipolaronic superconductivity out of a Coulomb gas. Figure 1 presents T c /Ω computed from Eq. ( 3) using m BP and R 2 BP obtained from QMC simulations of two fermions in the model as a function of λ for different t/Ω at U = 8t and V = U/10. Error bars in the figures represent one standard deviation statistical errors in QMC simulations. The results in Fig. 1 show that a sufficiently large value of λ is needed in 3D in order form bound states and obtain an s-wave bipolaronic superconductor in the strong correlation regime considered here. Note that, first, the choice of U/t = 8 implies strong competition between the onsite repulsion and the electronic kinetic energy, and, second, the ratio V /U = 1/10 is a reasonable estimate for typical materials such as the transition metal oxides, which have a roughly 10:1 ratio between the lattice constant (∼ 4 Å) and the orbital size (∼ 0.5 Å). Thus, our results in Fig. 1 prove that bipolaronic superconductivity is robust even in the presence of strong, poorly screened Coulomb repulsion, see Fig. 2 for more extensive results. To appreciate this result, we contrast in Fig. 1 T c /Ω to that of Holstein bipolarons [37,38] with electron-phonon coupling term given by Ve-ph = α √ 2Ω i,σ ĉ † i,σ ĉi,σ Xi . We find that T c of the bond-Peierls bipolaronic superconductor is generically higher than that in the Holstein model including in the adiabatic limit t > Ω. T c of the Holstein bipolaronic superconductor never exceeds ∼ 0.03Ω because binding requires sufficiently large λ which leads to either large size at criticality or rapid mass enhancement soon after and thus a low T c . In contrast, the bond-Peierls bipolaron becomes strongly bound but remains relatively light and small and therefore can accommodate the Coulomb repulsion, see Fig. 3. We note that the values of T c /Ω in this case appear to be comparable to or greater than the upper bound of 0.05 from McMillan's phenomenological approach to Migdal-Eliashberg theory in the adiabatic limit for moderate values of λ 1 [39]. In other words, our theory predicts values of T c in line with typical values found in experiments on dilute superconductors.

Having established bipolaronic superconductivity out of a Coulomb gas, we study in Fig. 2 the dependence of T c /Ω on V /U at fixed U/t (Fig. 2a) and on U/t at fixed V /U (Fig. 2b). The main results of Fig. 2 can be summarized as follows. First, we find long-range repulsioninduced reduction of T c , but, importantly, T c /Ω remains relatively large even for large V = U/10. This reflects the ability of the bipolaron wavefunction to spread itself effectively over multiple sites in order to accommodate the Coulomb repulsion, see inset of Fig. 3c. Second, onsite repulsion may enhance T c similar to what we found in 2D models with no long-range interaction [15]. This unconventional behavior follows from the ability of the bipolaron to effectively reduce its mass without significantly increasing its radius as U is increased. Our analysis thus shows that in presence of Coulomb repulsion bond bipolarons form with relatively light mass yet with small size and can benefit from the local repulsion by decreasing their mass without significantly increasing their size, hence proving that the bond bipolaronic superconductivity is generally much less sensitive to Coulomb repulsion than Holstein bipolarons, and can in fact take advantage of the local repulsion to increase T c .

Phenomenology of bipolaronic superconductivity. Figure 3 details the features of bipolarons in presence of long-range Coulomb repulsion. A much sharper dependence on the electron-phonon coupling becomes apparent for larger values of V as can be seen in the pairing temperature T p (Fig. 3a) defined as the bipolaron binding energy and in the bipolaron effective mass (Fig. 3b) and squared-radius (Fig. 3c). This behavior implies that breakdown of Fermi liquid theory due to bipolaronic collapse depends non-trivially on the interplay of electron-phonon and electron-electron interactions and goes beyond the current understanding of the breakdown of Migdal-Eliasberg theory due to a first-order transition driven by bipolaron formation [40][41][42][43][44][45][46][47][48]. Second, Fig. 3 demonstrates that a sufficiently large value of λ is needed in order to overcome the Coulomb repulsion and form bipolarons, but that mass enhancement (Fig. 3b) remains moderate (mass enhancement is much more dramatic in the Holstein case). We can also see from Fig. 3b,c that small-size bipolarons with R BP < 3 exhibit rather weak mass enhancement m BP < 10 explaining the large values of T c at optimized λ despite the strong Coulomb repulsion. Overall, T c drops from large values at V = 0 to values approximately 3 times smaller at V = U/10, see also Fig. 2. This modest reduction in T c reflects the ability of bipolarons to effectively accommodate the Coulomb repulsion by spreading their wavefunction effectively over multiple sites even at large λ, see the inset of Fig. 3c. In Fig. 4 we study the phase diagram of the bipolaronic superconductor in the T -λ space in the adiabatic limit in presence of large Coulomb repulsion. A bipolaronic BEC superconductor forms at T ≤ T c . There is an extended region at temperatures T c < T ≤ T p characterized by non-superconducting correlations in a normal gas of bipolarons, which will give rise to a large regime of phase fluctuations in experiment. Above T p , the bipolarons unbind into a polaron gas. Finally, in Fig. 5 we discuss the crossover or phase transition occurring as the density is increased into the BCS regime [49,50] in which 1 FIG. 4. Phase diagram in the T /⌦-space of the bo Peierls model in the adiabatic limit t/⌦ = 2 with strong on U/t = 8 and Coulomb V = U/10 interactions. A BEC sup conductor forms at T  T c (dark blue region). There large region at temperatures T c < T  T p characterized non-superconducting correlations and phase fluctuations normal gas of bipolarons (light blue region). Above T p , bipolarons unbind into a polaron gas (gray region).
limit for moderate values of . 1 [39]. In other wor our theory predicts values of T c in line with typical val found in experiments on dilute superconductors.

Having established bipolaronic superconductivity of a Coulomb gas, we study in Fig. 2 the dependence T c /⌦ on V /U at fixed U/t (Fig. 2a) and on U/t at fi V /U (Fig. 2b). The main results of Fig. 2 can be su marized as follows. First, we find long-range repulsi induced reduction of T c , but, importantly, T c /⌦ rema relatively large even for large V = U/10. This refle the ability of the bipolaron wavefunction to spread it e↵ectively over multiple sites in order to accommod the Coulomb repulsion, see inset of Fig. 3c. Second, site repulsion may enhance T c similar to what we fou in 2D models with no long-range interaction [15]. T unconventional behavior follows from the ability of bipolaron to e↵ectively reduce its mass without sign cantly increasing its radius as U is increased. Our an ysis thus shows that in presence of Coulomb repuls bond bipolarons form with relatively light mass yet w small size and can benefit from the local repulsion by creasing their mass without significantly increasing th size, hence proving that the bond bipolaronic superc ductivity is generally much less sensitive to Coulomb pulsion than Holstein bipolarons, and can in fact t advantage of the local repulsion to increase T c .

⌧ 1 Phenomenology of bipolaronic superconductivity. F ure 3 details the features of bipolarons in presence long-range Coulomb repulsion. A much sharper dep dence on the electron-phonon coupling becomes app ent for larger values of V as can be seen in the pair temperature T p (Fig. 3a) defined as the bipolaron bi ing energy and in the bipolaron e↵ective mass (Fig. andsquared-radius (Fig. 3c). This behavior implies t

The mechanisms of superconductivity in the dilute-density reg known that Bardeen-Cooper-Schrie↵er theory predicts a critical ponentially vanishing in the density of states, implying vanish carrier density limit. Alternatively, a Bose-Einstein condensate low densities, but this requires a strong phonon-mediated pairing Coulomb repulsion and bind electron pairs into bound states. Ho phonon-mediated pairing can lead to exponential mass enhancem Here, using exact sign-problem-free quantum Monte Carlo, we in Coulomb forces on BEC of bipolarons. We study a model of hopping which was recently shown to give rise to small-size, ligh superfluid transition at high values of Tc in absence of long-range to that of the standard model of phonon-modulated density coupl Tc in our model remains much larger than that of Holstein bipo or greater than the typical upper bounds on phonon-mediated T McMillan expectations. Our work points to a physically simple m the low-density regime that may be relevant to current experime Introduction. Understanding the mechanisms of superconductivity in the dilute density regime is an active theme of research, relevant to a number of materials, including the polar materials [1,2], doped topological insulators [3,4], transition metal dichalcogenides [5], moire materials [6][7][8][9][10][11] and others [12]. The Bardeen-Cooper-Schrie↵er (BCS) theory cannot explain superconductivity in this regime as it predicts that the critical transition temperature (T c ) below which superconductivity emerges must vanish exponentially as the electronic density of states 0. Proximity to a quantum critical point can enhance superconductivity at strong coupling even in the small density limit, but this does not explain how superconductivity arises and persists away from criticality. Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) of preformed pairs offers a robust route to superconductivity at low densities. In the low-density regime, the Coulomb repulsion is weakly screened and thus the pairing 'glue' required to bind electron pairs into bound states must be su ciently strong. However, a strong pairing interaction may result in heavy bound states and since T c is determined by the inverse of their e↵ective mass, this once again implies vanishing T c in the small-density limit. These considerations [13] are widely believed to be severe in the case of phonon-mediated binding of electrons into bipolarons, where it is usually assumed that whenever the phonon-med to overcome exponentiall Even in th perconducti pling in the electron hop assumed to in which w modulated e mass bipola sition at val tained in mo to phonons theory of su

H phonon-mediated pairing can lead to exponential mass enhanc Here, using exact sign-problem-free quantum Monte Carlo, we Coulomb forces on BEC of bipolarons. We study a model o hopping which was recently shown to give rise to small-size, li superfluid transition at high values of Tc in absence of long-rang to that of the standard model of phonon-modulated density coup Tc in our model remains much larger than that of Holstein bip or greater than the typical upper bounds on phonon-mediated McMillan expectations. Our work points to a physically simple the low-density regime that may be relevant to current experim Introduction. Understanding the mechanisms of superconductivity in the dilute density regime is an active theme of research, relevant to a number of materials, including the polar materials [1,2], doped topological insulators [3,4], transition metal dichalcogenides [5], moire materials [6][7][8][9][10][11] and others [12]. The Bardeen-Cooper-Schrie↵er (BCS) theory cannot explain superconductivity in this regime as it predicts that the critical transition temperature (T c ) below which superconductivity emerges must vanish exponentially as the electronic density of states 0. Proximity to a quantum critical point can enhance superconductivity at strong coupling even in the small density limit, but this does not explain how superconductivity arises and persists away from criticality. Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) of preformed pairs offers a robust route to superconductivity at low densities. In the low-density regime, the Coulomb repulsion is weakly screened and thus the pairing 'glue' required to bind electron pairs into bound states must be su ciently strong. However, a strong pairing interaction may result in heavy bound states and since T c is determined by the inverse of their e↵ective mass, this once again implies vanishing T c in the small-density limit. These considerations [13] are widely believed to be severe in the case of phonon-mediated binding of electrons into bipolarons, where it is usually assumed that whenever the phonon-me to overcom exponentia Even in perconduct pling in th electron ho assumed to in which modulated mass bipol sition at va tained in m to phonons theory of s J. Sous, Introduction. perconductivity i theme of research cluding the polar sulators [3,4], tra materials [6][7][8][9][10][11] Schrie↵er (BCS) ity in this regim sition temperatu emerges must van sity of states 0 can enhance supe the small density perconductivity a Bose-Einstein con fers a robust rou ties. In the low-d weakly screened bind electron pai strong. However sult in heavy bou the inverse of th plies vanishing T siderations [13]

div.head: a case of phonon-m larons, where it
div.p: The mechanisms of superconductivity in the dilute-density regime are highly debated. It is well known that Bardeen-Cooper-Schrie↵er theory predicts a critical transition temperature that is exponentially vanishing in the density of states, implying vanishing superconductivity in the low carrier density limit. Alternatively, a Bose-Einstein condensate-type superconductor can form at low densities, but this requires a strong phonon-mediated pairing potential in order to overcome the Coulomb repulsion and bind electron pairs into bound states. However, at large coupling strengths, phonon-mediated pairing can lead to exponential mass enhancement, hindering superconductivity. Here, using exact sign-problem-free quantum Monte Carlo, we investigate the e↵ects of long-range Coulomb forces on BEC of bipolarons. We study a model of bond phonon-modulated electron hopping which was recently shown to give rise to small-size, light-mass bipolarons that undergo a superfluid transition at high values of Tc in absence of long-range repulsion and contrast its physics to that of the standard model of phonon-modulated density coupling (Holstein model). We find that Tc in our model remains much larger than that of Holstein bipolarons, and can be on the order of or greater than the typical upper bounds on phonon-mediated Tc based on Migdal-Eliashberg and McMillan expectations. Our work points to a physically simple mechanism for superconductivity in the low-density regime that may be relevant to current experiments on dilute superconductors.

Introduction. Understanding the mechanisms of superconductivity in the dilute density regime is an active theme of research, relevant to a number of materials, including the polar materials [1,2], doped topological insulators [3,4], transition metal dichalcogenides [5], moire materials [6][7][8][9][10][11] and others [12]. The Bardeen-Cooper-Schrie↵er (BCS) theory cannot explain superconductivity in this regime as it predicts that the critical transition temperature (T c ) below which superconductivity emerges must vanish exponentially as the electronic density of states ! 0. Proximity to a quantum critical point can enhance superconductivity at strong coupling even in the small density limit, but this does not explain how superconductivity arises and persists away from criticality. Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) of preformed pairs offers a robust route to superconductivity at low densities. In the low-density regime, the Coulomb repulsion is weakly screened and thus the pairing 'glue' required to bind electron pairs into bound states must be su ciently strong. However, a strong pairing interaction may result in heavy bound states and since T c is determined by the inverse of their e↵ective mass, this once again implies vanishing T c in the small-density limit. These considerations [13] are widely believed to be severe in the case of phonon-mediated binding of electrons into bipolarons, where it is usually assumed that whenever the phonon-mediated attraction becomes su ciently strong to overcome the Coulomb repulsion it must give rise to exponentially heavy bipolarons and thus low values of T c .

Even in the absence of Coulomb repulsion, high-T c superconductivity driven by strong electron-phonon coupling in the adiabatic limit t/⌦ > 1 (where t is the electron hopping and ⌦ is the phonon frequency) was assumed to be impossible [14] until a recent work [15] in which we have demonstrated that bond phononmodulated electron hopping gives rise to small-size, lightmass bipolarons [16,17] that undergo a superfluid transition at values of T c that are much larger than those obtained in models in which the electron density is coupled to phonons (Holstein model) or from Migdal-Eliashberg theory of superconductivity out of a Fermi liquid even in

The mechanisms of superconductivity in the dilute-density regime are highly debated. It is well known that Bardeen-Cooper-Schrie↵er theory predicts a critical transition temperature that is exponentially vanishing in the density of states, implying vanishing superconductivity in the low carrier density limit. Alternatively, a Bose-Einstein condensate-type superconductor can form at low densities, but this requires a strong phonon-mediated pairing potential in order to overcome the Coulomb repulsion and bind electron pairs into bound states. However, at large coupling strengths, phonon-mediated pairing can lead to exponential mass enhancement, hindering superconductivity. Here, using exact sign-problem-free quantum Monte Carlo, we investigate the e↵ects of long-range Coulomb forces on BEC of bipolarons. We study a model of bond phonon-modulated electron hopping which was recently shown to give rise to small-size, light-mass bipolarons that undergo a superfluid transition at high values of Tc in absence of long-range repulsion and contrast its physics to that of the standard model of phonon-modulated density coupling (Holstein model). We find that Tc in our model remains much larger than that of Holstein bipolarons, and can be on the order of or greater than the typical upper bounds on phonon-mediated Tc based on Migdal-Eliashberg and McMillan expectations. Our work points to a physically simple mechanism for superconductivity in the low-density regime that may be relevant to current experiments on dilute superconductors.

Introduction. Understanding the mechanisms of superconductivity in the dilute density regime is an active theme of research, relevant to a number of materials, including the polar materials [1,2], doped topological insulators [3,4], transition metal dichalcogenides [5], moire materials [6][7][8][9][10][11] and others [12]. The Bardeen-Cooper-Schrie↵er (BCS) theory cannot explain superconductivity in this regime as it predicts that the critical transition temperature (T c ) below which superconductivity emerges must vanish exponentially as the electronic density of states ! 0. Proximity to a quantum critical point can enhance superconductivity at strong coupling even in the small density limit, but this does not explain how superconductivity arises and persists away from criticality. Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) of preformed pairs offers a robust route to superconductivity at low densities. In the low-density regime, the Coulomb repulsion is weakly screened and thus the pairing 'glue' required to bind electron pairs into bound states must be su ciently strong. However, a strong pairing interaction may result in heavy bound states and since T c is determined by the inverse of their e↵ective mass, this once again implies vanishing T c in the small-density limit. These considerations [13] are widely believed to be severe in the case of phonon-mediated binding of electrons into bipolarons, where it is usually assumed that whenever the phonon-mediated attraction becomes su ciently strong to overcome the Coulomb repulsion it must give rise to exponentially heavy bipolarons and thus low values of T c .

Even in the absence of Coulomb repulsion, high-T c superconductivity driven by strong electron-phonon coupling in the adiabatic limit t/⌦ > 1 (where t is the electron hopping and ⌦ is the phonon frequency) was assumed to be impossible [14] until a recent work [15] in which we have demonstrated that bond phononmodulated electron hopping gives rise to small-size, lightmass bipolarons [16,17] that undergo a superfluid transition at values of T c that are much larger than those obtained in models in which the electron density is coupled to phonons (Holstein model) or from Migdal-Eliashberg theory of superconductivity out of a Fermi liquid even in

The mechanisms of superconductivity in the dilute-density regime are highly debated. It is well known that Bardeen-Cooper-Schrie↵er theory predicts a critical transition temperature that is exponentially vanishing in the density of states, implying vanishing superconductivity in the low carrier density limit. Alternatively, a Bose-Einstein condensate-type superconductor can form at low densities, but this requires a strong phonon-mediated pairing potential in order to overcome the Coulomb repulsion and bind electron pairs into bound states. However, at large coupling strengths, phonon-mediated pairing can lead to exponential mass enhancement, hindering superconductivity. Here, using exact sign-problem-free quantum Monte Carlo, we investigate the e↵ects of long-range Coulomb forces on BEC of bipolarons. We study a model of bond phonon-modulated electron hopping which was recently shown to give rise to small-size, light-mass bipolarons that undergo a superfluid transition at high values of Tc in absence of long-range repulsion and contrast its physics to that of the standard model of phonon-modulated density coupling (Holstein model). We find that Tc in our model remains much larger than that of Holstein bipolarons, and can be on the order of or greater than the typical upper bounds on phonon-mediated Tc based on Migdal-Eliashberg and McMillan expectations. Our work points to a physically simple mechanism for superconductivity in the low-density regime that may be relevant to current experiments on dilute superconductors.

Introduction. Understanding the mechanisms of superconductivity in the dilute density regime is an active theme of research, relevant to a number of materials, including the polar materials [1,2], doped topological insulators [3,4], transition metal dichalcogenides [5], moire materials [6][7][8][9][10][11] and others [12]. The Bardeen-Cooper-Schrie↵er (BCS) theory cannot explain superconductivity in this regime as it predicts that the critical transition temperature (T c ) below which superconductivity emerges must vanish exponentially as the electronic density of states ! 0. Proximity to a quantum critical point can enhance superconductivity at strong coupling even in the small density limit, but this does not explain how superconductivity arises and persists away from criticality. Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) of preformed pairs offers a robust route to superconductivity at low densities. In the low-density regime, the Coulomb repulsion is weakly screened and thus the pairing 'glue' required to bind electron pairs into bound states must be su ciently strong. However, a strong pairing interaction may result in heavy bound states and since T c is determined by the inverse of their e↵ective mass, this once again implies vanishing T c in the small-density limit. These considerations [13] are widely believed to be severe in the case of phonon-mediated binding of electrons into bipolarons, where it is usually assumed that whenever the phonon-mediated attraction becomes su ci to overcome the Coulomb repulsion it must exponentially heavy bipolarons and thus low
div.formula: T c T BEC (2m p ) 2m p m ? BP 3 2⇡R 3 BP n 1
div.p: Even in the absence of Coulomb repulsion, perconductivity driven by strong electron-p pling in the adiabatic limit t/⌦ > 1 (wh electron hopping and ⌦ is the phonon freq assumed to be impossible [14] until a recen in which we have demonstrated that bo modulated electron hopping gives rise to sma mass bipolarons [16,17] that undergo a sup sition at values of T c that are much larger th tained in models in which the electron densit to phonons (Holstein model) or from Migda theory of superconductivity out of a Fermi li FIG. 5. Heavy black line: Superfluid transition temperature Tc of the bipolaronic gas normalized to the BEC temperature TBEC of a gas of bosons of density n and mass 2mp as a function of the inverse density n -1 normalized to the effective bipolaron density 3/(2πR 3 BP ). Dashed back line: Proposed extrapolation of Tc beyond the density at which the bipolarons overlap (black dot) and the BEC picture breaks down. As the density enters the n > 3/(2πR 3 BP ) BCS regime we expect, based on comparison to the unitary Fermi gas (dashed blue line), that the decrease in mass associated with unbinding of bipolarons into Fermi liquid may drive further increase in Tc. bipolarons overlap. A useful analogy is provided by the unitary Fermi gas (fermions in the continuum interacting with attractive contact interaction) picture of the BCS-BEC crossover [51,52] where in the dilute limit weakly bound fermion pairs with mass twice the bare fermion mass condense into a BEC and as the density is increased into the BCS regime of overlapping pairs T c drops only slowly. We may expect that the basic physics of the BCS-BEC crossover in the bipolaron system is similar to that for unitary fermions, but with the additional feature that the mass drops rapidly as the density increases beyond the point at which bipolarons overlap and unbind into polarons (with mass m p ) and then into moderately renormalized electrons. This density dependence of the mass, not present in the unitary Fermi gas, may lead to a further increase in T c as the density is increased into the BCS regime.

Conclusion. We have shown using an exact approach that Bose condensation of bond-coupled bipolarons out of a Coulomb gas provides a robust mechanism for superconductivity in the dilute limit, with a transition temperature that is generically larger than what is found in the Holstein model even in the presence of strong Coulomb repulsion (as estimated for the transition metal oxides for example). This transition temperature can be greater than or on the order of the heuristic bounds on phononmediated superconductivity based on McMillan theory, suggesting that our mechanism may already be operative in a number of materials. We show that the local onsite Coulomb repulsion enhances T c while the nonlocal part reduces T c , which, however, remains relatively high. The key ingredient of the combination of light mass and relatively small size of bipolarons even in presence of long-range Coulomb repulsion explains the robustness of the mechanism and the relatively large values of T c found. The crossover or phase transition to the BCS regime when bipolarons start to overlap is an open question, but, in contrast to the unitary Fermi gas [51,52], we cannot rule out that T c may have a peak as a function of carrier density in the crossover region where bipolarons unbind into quasiparticles with much smaller mass which would imply even larger values of T c on the BCS side.

figure.head: associated with the a b FIG. 2 .
figure.label: b2
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2. Estimates of Tc of the bond-Peierls bipolaronic superconductor for t/Ω = 2 as a function of λ = α 2 /(3Ωt), for a. different strengths of the Coulomb repulsion V at fixed onsite U/t = 8, and for b. different strengths of the onsite U at fixed long-range repulsion V = U/10, computed according to Eq. (3) from QMC simulations of the bipolaron effective mass m BP and mean squared-radius R 2 BP .

figure.head: FIG. 3 .
figure.figDesc: FIG.3. Bipolaron properties in the model, Eq. (1), at t/Ω = 2 as a function of λ = α 2 /(3Ωt) for different V at fixed U/t = 8: a. Bipolaron binding energy Tp, b. Bipolaron effective mass m BP in units of the mass of two free electrons m0 = 2me = 1/t, and c. Bipolaron squared-radius R 2 BP and, in the inset, its radial size probability density distribution PBP(R) at large coupling. Error bars in PBP(R) correspond to statistical errors smaller than the symbol size and therefore are not shown.

figure.head: 2 / 3 BP
figure.figDesc: /m BP , where ρ BP is the density of bipolarons and m BP

figure.head: 2 U 10 FIG. 4 .
figure.figDesc: FIG.4. Phase diagram in the T /Ω-λ space of the bond-Peierls model in the adiabatic limit t/Ω = 2 with strong onsite U/t = 8 and Coulomb V = U/10 interactions. A BEC superconductor forms at T ≤ Tc (dark blue region). There is a large region at temperatures Tc < T ≤ Tp characterized by non-superconducting correlations and phase fluctuations in a normal gas of bipolarons (light blue region). Above Tp, the bipolarons unbind into a polaron gas (gray region).

div.p: We acknowledge useful discussions with S. Kivelson and S. Raghu. J. S. acknowledges support from the Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation's EPiQS Initiative through Grant GBMF8686 at Stanford University. J. S., D. R. R. and A. J. M. acknowledge support from the National Science Foundation (NSF) Materials Research Science and Engineering Centers (MRSEC) program through Columbia University in the Center for Precision Assembly of Superstratic and Superatomic Solids under Grant No. DMR-1420634. C. Z. acknowledges support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Grant No. 12204173. M. B. acknowledges support from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC), the Stew-art Blusson Quantum Matter Institute (SBQMI) and the Max-Planck-UBC-UTokyo Center for Quantum Materials. B. V. S. and N. V. P acknowledge support from the NSF under Grant No. DMR-2032077. J. S. also acknowledges the hospitality of the Center for Computational Quantum Physics (CCQ) at the Flatiron Institute. The Flatiron Institute is a division of the Simons Foundation.
